{{基礎情報 神 | 名称 = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

{{基礎情報 神 | 名称 = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

{{基礎情報 神 | 名称 = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

{{shinto-stub|en}} [[Category:Deities of medicine]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Japanese Taoism]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yakusojin}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Medical Deity-qq]] [[Category:Yakusojin]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yakusojin}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Medical Deity-qq]] [[Category:Yakusojin]][[Category:Yakusojin]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:薬祖神]]

{{基礎情報 神 | 名称 = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

{{基礎情報 神 | 名称 = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>

'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>

{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Inaba no Shiro Usagi|ja|因幡の白兎|en}} * {{ill|Oyagami|ja|祖神|en}}

{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>

In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />

Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>

== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Inaba no Shiro Usagi|ja|因幡の白兎|en}} * {{ill|Oyagami|ja|祖神|en}}

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Inaba no Shiro Usagi|ja|因幡の白兎|en}} * {{ill|Oyagami|ja|祖神|en}}
{{shinto-stub|en}} [[Category:Deities of medicine]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Japanese Taoism]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yakusojin}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Medical Deity--> [[Category:Yakusojin]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yakusojin}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Medical Deity--> [[Category:Yakusojin]][[Category:Yakusojin]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:薬祖神]]

{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Inaba no Shiro Usagi|ja|因幡の白兎|en}} * {{ill|Oyagami|ja|祖神|en}}

{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}

They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>

Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
