{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|Image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>

In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Inaba no Shiro Usagi|ja|因幡の白兎|en}} * {{ill|Oyagami|ja|祖神|en}}

{{基礎情報 神 | |name=Medical Deity|native_name = Yakusojin (薬祖神) | 画像 = [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] | 説明 = |en}}
'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

'''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>

In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references />== See Also == * {{ill|Inaba no Shiro Usagi|ja|因幡の白兎|en}} * {{ill|Oyagami|ja|祖神|en}}
{{shinto-stub|en}} [[Category:Deities of medicine]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Japanese Taoism]] {{citation needed|Yakusojin}} [[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] <!--Medical Deity--> [[Category:Yakusojin]]{{citation needed|Yakusojin}} [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] <!--Medical Deity--> [[Category:Yakusojin]][[Category:Yakusojin]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[ja:薬祖神]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

[[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references />== See Also == * {{ill|Inaba no Shiro Usagi|ja|因幡の白兎|en}} * {{ill|Oyagami|ja|祖神|en}}
{{shinto-stub|en}} [[Category:Deities of medicine]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Japanese Taoism]] {{citation needed|Yakusojin}} [[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] <!--Medical Deity--> [[Category:Yakusojin]]{{citation needed|Yakusojin}} [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] <!--Medical Deity--> [[Category:Yakusojin]][[Category:Yakusojin]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:11]] [[ja:薬祖神]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Medical Deity]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

[[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|en}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|en}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[ファイル:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|サムネイル|吉田義之(1908年)『人寿説』より、「小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像」。]] '''薬祖神'''（やくそじん）は、医療の始祖と語り継がれる各種の神のことである。'''医祖神'''（いそしん）とも呼ばれる<ref name="isoshin"><cite id="isoshin"></cite>{{Cite web|和書 |url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html |title=SI062 医祖神碣 |author=フィールドミュージアム京都 |accessdate=2019-10-12}}</ref>。日本において伝統的に信仰されてきた薬祖神は、[[中国神話]]の[[神農]]であり、[[日本神話]]の[[大国主|大己貴神]]・[[スクナビコナ|少彦名神]]もまた同様に崇敬されることがあった<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|和書|author=水沢利忠|year=1987|title=農家の神農から医薬の神農まで|journal=斯文|volume=93|pages=12-31}}</ref>。
神農は古代中国の伝説上の帝王で、[[三皇]]のひとりであるとされているものの<ref>{{Kotobank |encyclopedia=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 |word=神農}}</ref>、おそらく当初は[[楚]]の地方神であったのではないかと考えられている<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|和書|author=[[芝哲夫]]|year=1997|title=杏雨蔵書にみる医学の歴史(1) ～神農像～|journal=実験治療|volume=649|pages=45-48}}</ref>。文献上の初出は『[[孟子 (書物)|孟子]]』であるが、これは[[戦国時代 (中国)|戦国時代]]、{{仮リンク|許行|en|Xu Xing (philosopher)}}なる人物が神農の教えに従い、民も君主もともに農耕に従事すべきであると主張したという記述であり、これにおける神農がいかなる人物であったかについては詳述されていない。[[漢|漢代]]の『[[緯書]]』には、神農は竜ないし牛の頭を有する奇怪な姿をしており、民に農業や養蚕、商業、そして医学を教えた人物であるという記述がある<ref>{{Kotobank |encyclopedia=日本大百科全書 |word=神農}}</ref>。『淮南子』には「始めて百草を嘗め、始めて医薬あり」との記述がある。[[司馬貞]]による『[[史記]]』三皇本紀には、神農はさまざまな毒や薬を舐めて作物や薬草の発見に勤め、農業および医学の始祖となったという旨の記述がなされている。こうして神農は薬祖神として信仰されるようになった。たとえば、[[晋 (王朝)|晋代]]の『[[帝王世紀]]』には、神農は医者と薬売の神であると記述されている<ref name=":1" />。
日本にも神農について記した医学書は渡来していたものの、少なくとも[[平安時代|平安期]]までにおいて神農を祭祀の対象とする伝統はなかったものとみられている。[[鎌倉幕府]]成立前後に輸入され、[[本草学]]の規範となった[[宋 (王朝)|宋]]版『{{仮リンク|証類本草|zh|证类本草}}』諸序には神農についての記述があり、[[小曽戸洋]]によれば、同書が日本における神農崇拝の契機となったのではないかと考えられる。また、同時期に日本に普及した『[[太平御覧]]』『[[医説]]』といった中国本草書にも神農についての記載があった。[[弘安]]7年（[[1284年]]）の『[[医談抄]]』には神農が本草の祖として明記され、同10年（[[1287年]]）には現存する最古の神農画である『[[馬医草紙]]』が描かれている。{{仮リンク|王履|zh|王履}}は[[1367年]]に『[[医経溯洞集]]』を執筆し、その巻頭に「神農嘗百草論」をおいたが、この書は中国のみならず日本や朝鮮をふくむ東アジア圏において、神農の薬祖神としての性質を強調させるうえで大きな役割を果たした。同書は[[熊宗立]]が上梓した医学叢書であり、日本にも多く輸入された『[[東垣十書]]』にも収録され、[[室町期]]から近世における日本医学を基礎づけるものとなった。また、熊が出版した『[[歴代名医図讃]]』は、日本における神農画流行のきっかけとなった。[[安土桃山時代|安土桃山期]]から[[江戸時代|江戸期]]にかけての日本では神農画を描く文化が定着し、特に江戸期においてはおびただしい数の神農画が描かれた<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=小曽戸洋|year=1994|title=近世日本の医薬界における神農画賛流行の背景|journal=日本医史学雑誌|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|ref=}}</ref>。また、日本各地に[[神農講]]が形成され、[[神農祭]]がとりおこなわれた<ref name=":1" />。
大己貴神・少彦名神は日本神話の神であり、『[[日本書紀]]』には両者が協力し、民と家畜の病の治療法を定めたという旨の記述がある<ref>{{Cite wikisource|title=日本書紀/卷第一|author=舍人親王|wslanguage=zh|quote=夫大己貴命與少彥名命。戮力一心。經營天下。復爲顯見蒼生及畜產。則定其療病之方。}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=木村彥右衞門|year=1932|title=藥祖神としての少彥命の位置|journal=本草|volume=2|pages=40-43}}</ref>。この2神を祭神とする[[大洗磯前神社]]や[[五條天神社 (京都市)|五條天神社]]は薬種業者の崇敬を集めてきた<ref name=":0" />。[[大阪市|大阪]]・[[道修町]]の[[少彦名神社 (大阪市)|少彦名神社]]は[[安永 (元号)|安永]]9年（[[1780年]]）に同地の薬種業者が五條天神社を分霊して建立した神社であり、祭神として少彦名神および神農を祀っている<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=加藤紫識|year=2010|title=日本の都市における同業者集団の信仰(第394回例会報告)|journal=比較都市史研究|volume=29|issue=1|pages=2-3}}</ref>。また、特異な例では、「[[西洋医学]]の祖」たる[[古代ギリシア]]の[[ヒポクラテス]]も加えられることがある（[[ヒポクラテス崇拝]]）<ref>京都ゆうゆう倶楽部{{Google books|bppklbN02WUC|『京都人が書いた「京都」の本 名所・旧跡からお土産・風習まで』|page=22}} [[PHP文庫]]、2008年8月1日。</ref>。
== 主な神社 == * [[五條天神社 (京都市)|五條天神社]]（[[京都府]][[京都市]][[下京区]]） - 「皇國醫祖神五條天神宮」・「醫祖神之碣（いそしんのけつ）」という石碑が立つ<ref name="isoshin"/>。 * [[五條天神社 (台東区)|五條天神社]]（[[東京都]][[台東区]][[上野恩賜公園#上野公園（町名）|上野公園]]）<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html |title=薬祖神の由来 |publisher=東京薬事協会 |accessdate=2019-10-12}}</ref> - 上記からの[[分霊|勧請]]。 * [[薬祖神社]]（[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]・[[菅原神社 (堺市)|菅原神社]]摂社） - 少彦名命とともに神農大神を祀る。「日本最古の薬祖神社」と称し、日本における神農信仰としては最古級に類する<ref name="sakaitenjin"><cite id="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|title=えべっさんと神農さん｜堺戎神社・堺薬祖神社｜菅原神社|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>。 * [[少彦名神社 (大阪市)|少彦名神社]]（大阪府[[大阪市]][[中央区 (大阪市)|中央区]]道修町） - 「道修町の神農さん」として知られる。少彦名命とともに神農炎帝を祀る。 * [[薬祖神社 (東京都)|薬祖神社]]（[[東京都]][[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]][[日本橋室町]]） - [[日本橋室町東地区開発]]により、日本橋にあった[[薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|薬祖神社]]を移転勧進した<ref name="fukutoku"><cite id="fukutoku">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|publisher=三井不動産|title=人々が集い日本の自然と日本橋の歴史と伝統を再現した憩いの空間 『福徳の森』 9月28日(水)誕生|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>。 * [[生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|生根神社]]（大阪府大阪市[[住吉区]]住吉） - 少彦名命を単独かつ主祭神として祀る神社としては最古級<ref name="ikune"><cite id="ikune">{{Cite book|和書|editor=式内社研究会編|author=奥野茂寿|year=1977|chapter=生根神社|title=式内社調査報告 第5巻|publisher=[[皇學館大学]]出版部|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|生根神社（式内社）|1977年}}}}</cite></ref>。 * [[生根神社 (大阪市西成区)|生根神社]]（大阪府大阪市[[西成区]][[玉出]]） - 上記からの[[分霊|勧請]]。 * [[大洗磯前神社]]（[[茨城県]][[東茨城郡]][[大洗町]]）<ref name="yakujikyo"/> * [[酒列磯前神社]]（茨城県[[ひたちなか市]]磯崎町）<ref name="yakujikyo"/> * [[大神神社#摂社|狭井神社・磐座神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]三輪・[[大神神社]]摂社） - 当社の前には「くすり道」がある<ref name="oomiwa"><cite id="oomiwa">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://oomiwa.or.jp/keidaimap/13-kusurimichi/|title=くすり道｜大神神社（おおみわじんじゃ）|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>。 * [[鴨都波神社]]摂社神農社（奈良県[[御所市]]宮前町） - 近隣に[[生薬]]栽培所<ref name="tamura"><cite id="tamura">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tamura-p.co.jp/yakuso/|title=田村薬草園|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>や製薬業者が多く、関係者に信仰されている<ref name="kamotsuba"><cite id="kamotsuba">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jptravel.net/2014/07/post_888.html|title=神農社が鎮まる鴨都波神社|publisher=大神神社御用達の大正楼|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref><ref name="mnjr05gob"><cite id="mnjr05gob">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://blog.goo.ne.jp/mnjr05gob/e/aae420a2c8f96ab417196d253eb6f07c|title=御所市宮前町神農社の薬祖神祭 - マネジャーの休日余暇（ブログ版）|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>。 * [[湯島聖堂]]境内神農廟（東京都[[文京区]][[湯島]]）<ref name="seido"><cite id="seido">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.seido.or.jp/cl02/detail-10.html|title=神農祭｜伝統行事｜史跡湯島聖堂｜公益財団法人斯文会|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[因幡の白兎]] * [[祖神]]

[[ファイル:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|サムネイル|吉田義之(1908年)『人寿説』より、「小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像」。]] '''薬祖神'''（やくそじん）は、医療の始祖と語り継がれる各種の神のことである。'''医祖神'''（いそしん）とも呼ばれる<ref name="isoshin"><cite id="isoshin"></cite>{{Cite web|和書 |url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html |title=SI062 医祖神碣 |author=フィールドミュージアム京都 |accessdate=2019-10-12}}</ref>。日本において伝統的に信仰されてきた薬祖神は、[[中国神話]]の[[神農]]であり、[[日本神話]]の[[大国主|大己貴神]]・[[スクナビコナ|少彦名神]]もまた同様に崇敬されることがあった<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|和書|author=水沢利忠|year=1987|title=農家の神農から医薬の神農まで|journal=斯文|volume=93|pages=12-31}}</ref>。

== 主な神社 == * [[五條天神社 (京都市)|五條天神社]]（[[京都府]][[京都市]][[下京区]]） - 「皇國醫祖神五條天神宮」・「醫祖神之碣（いそしんのけつ）」という石碑が立つ<ref name="isoshin"/>。 * [[五條天神社 (台東区)|五條天神社]]（[[東京都]][[台東区]][[上野恩賜公園#上野公園（町名）|上野公園]]）<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html |title=薬祖神の由来 |publisher=東京薬事協会 |accessdate=2019-10-12}}</ref> - 上記からの[[分霊|勧請]]。 * [[薬祖神社]]（[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]・[[菅原神社 (堺市)|菅原神社]]摂社） - 少彦名命とともに神農大神を祀る。「日本最古の薬祖神社」と称し、日本における神農信仰としては最古級に類する<ref name="sakaitenjin"><cite id="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|title=えべっさんと神農さん｜堺戎神社・堺薬祖神社｜菅原神社|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>。 * [[少彦名神社 (大阪市)|少彦名神社]]（大阪府[[大阪市]][[中央区 (大阪市)|中央区]]道修町） - 「道修町の神農さん」として知られる。少彦名命とともに神農炎帝を祀る。 * [[薬祖神社 (東京都)|薬祖神社]]（[[東京都]][[中央区 (東京都)|中央区]][[日本橋室町]]） - [[日本橋室町東地区開発]]により、日本橋にあった[[薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|薬祖神社]]を移転勧進した<ref name="fukutoku"><cite id="fukutoku">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|publisher=三井不動産|title=人々が集い日本の自然と日本橋の歴史と伝統を再現した憩いの空間 『福徳の森』 9月28日(水)誕生|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>。 * [[生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|生根神社]]（大阪府大阪市[[住吉区]]住吉） - 少彦名命を単独かつ主祭神として祀る神社としては最古級<ref name="ikune"><cite id="ikune">{{Cite book|和書|editor=式内社研究会編|author=奥野茂寿|year=1977|chapter=生根神社|title=式内社調査報告 第5巻|publisher=[[皇學館大学]]出版部|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|生根神社（式内社）|1977年}}}}</cite></ref>。 * [[生根神社 (大阪市西成区)|生根神社]]（大阪府大阪市[[西成区]][[玉出]]） - 上記からの[[分霊|勧請]]。 * [[大洗磯前神社]]（[[茨城県]][[東茨城郡]][[大洗町]]）<ref name="yakujikyo"/> * [[酒列磯前神社]]（茨城県[[ひたちなか市]]磯崎町）<ref name="yakujikyo"/> * [[大神神社#摂社|狭井神社・磐座神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]三輪・[[大神神社]]摂社） - 当社の前には「くすり道」がある<ref name="oomiwa"><cite id="oomiwa">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://oomiwa.or.jp/keidaimap/13-kusurimichi/|title=くすり道｜大神神社（おおみわじんじゃ）|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>。 * [[鴨都波神社]]摂社神農社（奈良県[[御所市]]宮前町） - 近隣に[[生薬]]栽培所<ref name="tamura"><cite id="tamura">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tamura-p.co.jp/yakuso/|title=田村薬草園|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>や製薬業者が多く、関係者に信仰されている<ref name="kamotsuba"><cite id="kamotsuba">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jptravel.net/2014/07/post_888.html|title=神農社が鎮まる鴨都波神社|publisher=大神神社御用達の大正楼|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref><ref name="mnjr05gob"><cite id="mnjr05gob">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://blog.goo.ne.jp/mnjr05gob/e/aae420a2c8f96ab417196d253eb6f07c|title=御所市宮前町神農社の薬祖神祭 - マネジャーの休日余暇（ブログ版）|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>。 * [[湯島聖堂]]境内神農廟（東京都[[文京区]][[湯島]]）<ref name="seido"><cite id="seido">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.seido.or.jp/cl02/detail-10.html|title=神農祭｜伝統行事｜史跡湯島聖堂｜公益財団法人斯文会|accessdate=2019-10-12}}</cite></ref>
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[因幡の白兎]] * [[祖神]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やくそしん}} [[Category:医神]] [[Category:神道]] [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:日本の道教]]

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Japanese Taoism]]
revision.text:
[[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong|qq=}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|ja|三皇五帝|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|qq=}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius (book)|ja|孟子|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mencius (book)|qq=}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|qq=}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|ja|淮南子|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Huainanzi|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|ja|史記|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiji|qq=}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|ja|太平御覧|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiping Yulan|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|qq=}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|qq=}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Inaba no Shiro Usagi|ja|因幡の白兎|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba no Shiro Usagi|qq=}} * {{ill|Oyagami|ja|祖神|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub|en}}
{{translated page|ja|薬祖神|version=101319868|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Deities of medicine]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese deities]]
[[Category:Completion tier 6]]
[[Category:Yakusojin]] }}
[[sn:Medical Deity]] [[ja:薬祖神]]

[[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] from Chinese mythology and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the [[:en:Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors]].<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the [[:en:Mencius|Mencius (book)]], where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|qq=}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''[[:en:Huainanzi|Huainanzi]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiji|Shiji]]'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''[[:en:Taiping Yulan|Taiping Yulan]]'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|qq=}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|qq=}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Hare of Inaba|Inaba no Shiro Usagi]] * [[:en:Oyagami|Oyagami]]

[[sn:Medical Deity]] [[ja:薬祖神]]
<!--Medical Deity--><!--Medical Deity-->

[[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] from Chinese mythology and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the [[:en:Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors]].<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the [[:en:Mencius|Mencius (book)]], where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|qq=}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''[[:en:Huainanzi|Huainanzi]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiji|Shiji]]'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''[[:en:Taiping Yulan|Taiping Yulan]]'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|qq=}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|qq=}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Hare of Inaba|Inaba no Shiro Usagi]] * [[:en:Oyagami|Oyagami]]

revision.text:
[[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] from Chinese mythology and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the [[:en:Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors]].<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the [[:en:Mencius|Mencius (book)]], where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|qq=}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''[[:en:Huainanzi|Huainanzi]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiji|Shiji]]'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''[[:en:Taiping Yulan|Taiping Yulan]]'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|qq=}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|qq=}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Hare of Inaba|Inaba no Shiro Usagi]] * [[:en:Oyagami|Oyagami]]

They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] from Chinese mythology and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>

By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|qq=}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>

In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|qq=}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|qq=}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|qq=}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Hare of Inaba|Inaba no Shiro Usagi]] * [[:en:Oyagami|Oyagami]]
