They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] from Chinese mythology and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the [[:en:Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors]].<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the [[:en:Mencius|Mencius (book)]], where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''[[:en:Huainanzi|Huainanzi]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiji|Shiji]]'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''[[:en:Taiping Yulan|Taiping Yulan]]'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|1=Doshōmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641619}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464750}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Gojō Tenjin Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372505}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464751}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Hare of Inaba|Inaba no Shiro Usagi]] * [[:en:Oyagami|Oyagami]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11622504]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] from Chinese mythology and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the [[:en:Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors]].<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the [[:en:Mencius|Mencius (book)]], where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''[[:en:Huainanzi|Huainanzi]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiji|Shiji]]'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''[[:en:Taiping Yulan|Taiping Yulan]]'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|1=Doshōmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641619}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464750}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Gojō Tenjin Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372505}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464751}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Hare of Inaba|Inaba no Shiro Usagi]] * [[:en:Oyagami|Oyagami]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11622504]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] from Chinese mythology and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the [[:en:Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors]].<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the [[:en:Mencius|Mencius (book)]], where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''[[:en:Huainanzi|Huainanzi]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiji|Shiji]]'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''[[:en:Taiping Yulan|Taiping Yulan]]'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|1=Doshōmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641619}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464750}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Gojō Tenjin Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372505}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464751}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Hare of Inaba|Inaba no Shiro Usagi]] * [[:en:Oyagami|Oyagami]]
{{shinto-stub|en}}
{{translated page|ja|薬祖神|version=101319868|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11622504]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] from Chinese mythology and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the [[:en:Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors]].<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the [[:en:Mencius|Mencius (book)]], where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''[[:en:Huainanzi|Huainanzi]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiji|Shiji]]'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''[[:en:Taiping Yulan|Taiping Yulan]]'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|1=Doshōmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641619}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464750}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Gojō Tenjin Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372505}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464751}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Hare of Inaba|Inaba no Shiro Usagi]] * [[:en:Oyagami|Oyagami]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11622504]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] from Chinese mythology and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the [[:en:Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors]].<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the [[:en:Mencius|Mencius (book)]], where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''[[:en:Huainanzi|Huainanzi]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiji|Shiji]]'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''[[:en:Taiping Yulan|Taiping Yulan]]'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|1=Doshōmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641619}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464750}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Gojō Tenjin Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372505}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464751}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * [[:en:Hare of Inaba|Inaba no Shiro Usagi]] * [[:en:Oyagami|Oyagami]]
{{shinto-stub|en}}
{{translated page|ja|薬祖神|version=101319868|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11622504]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Oarai Isosaki Shrine|en|Oarai Isosaki Shrine|WD=Q11437230|lt=Ōarai Isosaki Shrine}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|1=Doshōmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641619}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464750}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Gojō Tenjin Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372505}} (Kyoto) - <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11622504]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|en|Shennong|WD=Q313336|lt=Shennong}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|en|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|WD=Q201727|lt=Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius|en|Mencius|WD=Q188903|lt=Mencius (book)}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|en|Huainanzi|WD=Q590337|lt=Huainanzi}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|en|Shiji|WD=Q272530|lt=Shiji}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|en|Taiping Yulan|WD=Q586049|lt=Taiping Yulan}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Oarai Isosaki Shrine|en|Oarai Isosaki Shrine|WD=Q11437230|lt=Ōarai Isosaki Shrine}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|1=Doshōmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641619}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464750}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Gojō Tenjin Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372505}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464751}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hare of Inaba|en|Hare of Inaba|WD=Q2330988|lt=Inaba no Shiro Usagi}} * {{ill|Oyagami|en|Oyagami|WD=Q85882782|lt=Oyagami}}
{{shinto-stub|en}}
{{translated page|ja|薬祖神|version=101319868|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:薬祖神]]

Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464751}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hare of Inaba|en|Hare of Inaba|WD=Q2330988|lt=Inaba no Shiro Usagi}} * {{ill|Oyagami|en|Oyagami|WD=Q85882782|lt=Oyagami}}
{{shinto-stub|en}}

revision.comment: adding dummy category to make categories work
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]]
[[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|en|Shennong|WD=Q313336|lt=Shennong}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|en|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|WD=Q201727|lt=Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius|en|Mencius|WD=Q188903|lt=Mencius (book)}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|en|Huainanzi|WD=Q590337|lt=Huainanzi}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|en|Shiji|WD=Q272530|lt=Shiji}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|en|Taiping Yulan|WD=Q586049|lt=Taiping Yulan}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Oarai Isosaki Shrine|en|Oarai Isosaki Shrine|WD=Q11437230|lt=Ōarai Isosaki Shrine}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|1=Doshōmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641619}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464750}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>
== Notable Shrines == * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|五條天神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Kyoto)|1=Gojō Tenjin Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372505}} (Kyoto) - Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464751}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hare of Inaba|en|Hare of Inaba|WD=Q2330988|lt=Inaba no Shiro Usagi}} * {{ill|Oyagami|en|Oyagami|WD=Q85882782|lt=Oyagami}}
{{shinto-stub|en}}
{{translated page|ja|薬祖神|version=101319868|comment=Imported full ja history}}

A branch shrine of the Kyoto location.<ref name="yakujikyo">{{Cite web|title=The Origins of Yakusojin|publisher=Tokyo Pharmaceutical Association|url=http://www.yakujikyo.or.jp/festival/history.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja|薬祖神社 (堺市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Sakai)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka) - Claims to be the oldest Yakusojin shrine in Japan.<ref name="sakaitenjin">{{Cite web|title=Sakai Tenjin and Yakusojin Shrines|url=http://www.sakaitenjin.or.jp/original3.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社 (大阪市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Sukunahikona Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464751}} (Osaka) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:薬祖神]]
[[File:小彦名命、炎帝神農氏及びヒポクラテスの肖像.jpg|thumb|image of Sukuna Hikona no Mikoto, Emperor Shennong, and Hippocrates from Yoshida Yoshiyuki's ''Jinjusetu'' (1908).]] '''Yakusojin''' (薬祖神) refers to deities regarded as the founders of medicine. They are also called '''Isoshin''' (医祖神).<ref name="isoshin">{{Cite web|title=SI062 医祖神碣|author=Field Museum Kyoto|url=https://www2.city.kyoto.lg.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/si062.html|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> In Japan, traditionally venerated Yakusojin include {{ill|Shennong|en|Shennong|WD=Q313336|lt=Shennong|1=Shennong}} from Chinese mythology and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} and [[Sukunabikona]] from Japanese mythology.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|author=Mizusawa Toshitada|year=1987|title=From Agricultural Deity Shennong to Medical Deity Shennong|journal=Sibun|volume=93|pages=12-31|en}}</ref>
== Overview == Shennong is a legendary ruler from ancient Chinese mythology and one of the {{ill|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|en|Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|WD=Q201727|lt=Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors|1=Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors}}.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Britannica International Encyclopedia|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Initially, he was likely a local deity from Chu.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|author=Shiba Tetsuo|year=1997|title=Shennong Iconography in Kyū Shochu Medical Texts|journal=Experimental Therapy|volume=649|pages=45-48|en}}</ref> Shennong is first mentioned in the {{ill|Mencius|en|Mencius|WD=Q188903|lt=Mencius (book)|1=Mencius}}, where he is cited in the context of {{ill|Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja|許行|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xu Xing (philosopher)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} advocating that rulers and people alike should farm.<ref>{{Kotobank|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia Nipponica|word=Shennong|en}}</ref> Later texts, such as the ''{{ill|Huainanzi|en|Huainanzi|WD=Q590337|lt=Huainanzi|1=Huainanzi}}'' and ''{{ill|Shiji|en|Shiji|WD=Q272530|lt=Shiji|1=Shiji}}'', describe Shennong as tasting hundreds of herbs and teaching agriculture, medicine, and commerce.
In Japan, Chinese medical texts such as ''{{ill|Zhenglei Bencao|zh|证类本草|fr|Zheng lei bencao|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhenglei Bencao|qq=}}'' introduced Shennong as a deity of medicine. The spread of these texts, along with works like ''{{ill|Taiping Yulan|en|Taiping Yulan|WD=Q586049|lt=Taiping Yulan|1=Taiping Yulan}}'' and ''{{ill|Yixue|zh|医説|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yixue|qq=}}'', established Shennong's role as a Yakusojin. By the Kamakura period, Shennong became a central figure in Japanese medicine.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Ozoto Hiroshi|year=1994|title=The Cultural Spread of Shennong Iconography in Early Modern Japan|journal=Japanese Medical History Journal|volume=40|issue=3|pages=333-334|en}}</ref> His veneration led to the creation of various Shennong paintings and festivals, such as the {{ill|Shennong Festival|ja|神農祭|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shennong Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name=":1" />
Similarly, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona from Japanese mythology are credited with devising methods to treat illnesses in both humans and livestock.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Kimura Hikoemon|year=1932|title=The Role of Sukuna Hikona as a Yakusojin|journal=Materia Medica|volume=2|pages=40-43|en}}</ref> Temples like {{ill|Oarai Isosaki Shrine|en|Oarai Isosaki Shrine|WD=Q11437230|lt=Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|1=Oarai Isosaki Shrine}} and {{ill|Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|ja|五條天神社 (京都市)|1=Gojō Tenjin-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjinsha (Kyoto City)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Tenjin-gū|qq=}} are dedicated to these deities.<ref name=":0" />
In Osaka's {{ill|Doshomachi|ja|道修町|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doshomachi|1=Doshōmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641619}}, the {{ill|Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|ja|少彦名神社|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona Shrine (Osaka)|1=Q11464750|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464750}} was established in 1780, enshrining both Sukuna Hikona and Shennong. Interestingly, Hippocrates, considered the "Father of Western Medicine," has also been incorporated into this tradition.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Life: From Landmarks to Customs|author=Kyoto Yu-Yu Club|date=2008-08-01|en}}</ref>

Features the "Isoshin Memorial Stone." * {{ill|Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|五條天神社 (台東区)|1=Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo Tenjin Shrine (Tokyo)|qq=}} (Tokyo) - Known as "Doshomachi's Shennong Shrine." * {{ill|Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja|薬祖神社 (東京都中央区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakusojin Shrine (Tokyo)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Tokyo) - Relocated during redevelopment.<ref name="fukutoku">{{Cite web|title=Rebirth of Fukutoku Forest|publisher=Mitsui Fudosan|url=https://www.mitsuifudosan.co.jp/corporate/news/2016/0928/|accessdate=2019-10-12|en}}</ref> * {{ill|Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|ja|生根神社 (大阪市住吉区)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikune Shrine (Sumiyoshi)|1=Ikune Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574575}} (Osaka) - One of the oldest shrines dedicated solely to Sukuna Hikona.<ref name="ikune">{{Cite book|title=Report on Shrines Vol. 5|author=Okuno Shigehisa|year=1977|publisher=Kogakkan University|ref={{Harvid|Ikune Shrine (Report on Shrines)|1977|en}}|en}}</ref>
